commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=3951f559ce6fa649dd89745682ce89e7637da8bc)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds configured `maxTokens`/`contextWindow` lookup in the OpenAI embedding provider and tests that `provider.maxInputTokens` reflects that metadata.

PR surface: Source +27, Tests +44. Total +71 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source inspection gives a high-confidence reproduction of the patch gap: custom OpenAI-compatible provider ids resolve to the generic adapter while the PR only changes the OpenAI memory adapter. A live SiliconFlow HTTP 400 was not reproduced in this review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Contributor action is needed to repair the runtime-path bug and provide real behavior proof; this is not safe to route to ClawSweeper repair while external setup proof remains required.

target.title: fix(openai): honor configured embedding model input limits
target.body:
## Change Type / Scope / Linked Issue

- Type: fix
- Scope: `extensions/openai`, memory embeddings
- Linked Issue: fixes #91396

## Motivation

Fix memory indexing failures for third-party OpenAI-compatible embedding models whose real input limit is smaller than OpenClaw’s generic fallback.

Before this change, the OpenAI memory embedding provider only applied explicit input limits for a small built-in set of native OpenAI embedding models. When a third-party OpenAI-compatible embedding model was configured, the provider usually exposed no `maxInputTokens`, so downstream chunk enforcement fell back to the generic `8192` token default.

That is too permissive for providers such as SiliconFlow serving models like `BAAI/bge-large-zh-v1.5`, where the effective request limit can be much smaller. In that setup, oversized chunks could be sent upstream and memory indexing could fail with HTTP 400.

The issue author’s follow-up analysis narrowed the root cause well: this was not primarily a guarded-fetch transport problem, but a model-limit resolution problem.

## Changes

### Configured embedding limit fallback

#### `extensions/openai/embedding-provider.ts`

Added `resolveConfiguredModelMaxInputTokens(...)`.

When the embedding model is not one of the built-in OpenAI entries in `OPENAI_MAX_INPUT_TOKENS`, the provider now:

- looks up the configured provider in `config.models.providers`
- finds the matching configured model entry
- prefers configured `maxTokens`
- falls back to configured `contextWindow`

The resolved value is then exposed as `provider.maxInputTokens`, so existing memory chunk enforcement can honor the configured model limit before remote embedding requests are sent.

### Preserve existing OpenAI-native behavior

#### `extensions/openai/embedding-provider.ts`

Kept the current hardcoded limits for known native OpenAI embedding models.

The new config-based path only applies when no built-in OpenAI limit is available.

### Regression coverage

#### `extensions/openai/embedding-provider.test.ts`

Added focused tests covering:

- third-party OpenAI-compatible embedding models inheriting configured `maxTokens`
- fallback to configured `contextWindow` when `maxTokens` is not present

## Real behavior proof

### Behavior or issue addressed

Fixes memory embedding request sizing for third-party OpenAI-compatible embedding providers so configured model metadata is used to limit chunk size before remote embedding requests are sent.

This addresses the class of failures reported in #91396, where memory indexing could send oversized inputs to providers such as SiliconFlow and receive HTTP 400 responses.

### Real environment tested

- Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux
- Real repository code from this patched branch
- Local Node runtime used for repository test execution

### Exact steps or command run after this patch

Ran the targeted embedding provider test suite:

```bash
cd /root/openclaw
node /root/openclaw/scripts/run-vitest.mjs /root/openclaw/extensions/openai/embedding-provider.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix
Targeted test run from the patched checkout completed successfully:

$ node /root/openclaw/scripts/run-vitest.mjs /root/openclaw/extensions/openai/embedding-provider.test.ts
[exit code 0]

Observed result after fix
The OpenAI memory embedding provider now derives input limits from configured model metadata for third-party OpenAI-compatible embedding models instead of silently falling back to the generic 8192 token default.

That lets downstream memory chunk enforcement split inputs to the configured limit before remote embedding requests are sent.

What was not tested
I did not run a live SiliconFlow credentialed end-to-end indexing reproduction in this environment
I did not exhaustively validate every third-party OpenAI-compatible embedding backend
I did not run the full memory indexing pipeline against an external provider from this environment
Proof limitations or environment constraints
This patch is intentionally narrow and stays inside the provider-side limit resolution path. The verification focuses on the exact code path changed here: resolving and exposing maxInputTokens for configured third-party embedding models.

Because this environment did not have a live external SiliconFlow setup available, proof is based on targeted regression tests for the affected provider behavior rather than a credentialed remote indexing session.

Why this fix shape
This patch avoids hardcoding a SiliconFlow-only special case.

Instead, it uses model metadata the operator already configured under models.providers.*.models[], which makes the fix more generally useful for other OpenAI-compatible embedding providers with non-default request limits.

It also preserves existing behavior for native OpenAI embedding models by keeping the current built-in limit map as the first priority.

Test plan

 Run targeted OpenAI embedding provider tests

 Verify third-party configured maxTokens is honored

 Verify fallback to configured contextWindow
target.author: pommelle
